title: After the Wrong-Site Line
desc: In orthopedics, the team marked the wrong knee on the consent form. Surgery was stopped in time — no incision. The patient is still under anesthesia in the holding area.

patient.name: Omar
patient.condition: Scheduled knee arthroscopy — near miss, no harm incurred

law.text: The physician owes the patient loyal, clear information material to understanding their situation — including serious process failures that affect trust and future decisions.

complications.title: Chief asks minimal charting
complications.liveLine: "Document 'consent clarified pre-incision' — full detail invites lawsuits. He wasn't cut; no harm, no story."

complications.title: Circulating nurse shaken
complications.premise: She worries the team will blame her for sign-in.
complications.liveLine: "I said the site out loud — everyone rushed. If this disappears from the record, I'll lose faith."

complications.title: Spouse demands 'truth'
complications.liveLine: "Something went wrong — I know it. Tell me before he wakes up or I'll go to the press."

complications.title: COD request
complications.premise: Coder asks for clean diagnosis line.
complications.liveLine: "If you list 'wrong-site near miss,' preauth may void — can we keep it generic?"

steps.msg: Attending whispers: "We caught it. He's stable. Do we wake him and tell him everything now, or wait until he's clear-headed tomorrow?"
steps.hint: Transparency soon; timing should not be used to hide:

choices.label: Plan disclosure as soon as clinically safe today
choices.reaction: When I wake, I want the truth before rumors spread.

choices.label: Defer indefinitely to protect anxiety
choices.reaction: I always suspected you hid something — trust died slowly.

choices.label: Tell only "procedure delayed"
choices.reaction: Half-truth feels like fraud when I learn more later.

choices.label: File internal incident only
choices.reaction: Systems improved — but I wasn't respected as a person.

steps.msg: Omar wakes groggy but oriented. He asks: "Why is my surgery band on the other leg? Did you operate on the wrong knee?"
steps.hint: Plain facts, apology for process failure, clarify no surgical harm:

choices.label: Clear facts + apology + next steps
choices.reaction: I'm furious — but I believe you stopped in time.

choices.label: Blame the resident quietly
choices.reaction: Whispers say you lied about who failed — I trust no one.

steps.msg: Omar wants reschedule with a different surgeon, and asks the hospital to pay for anxiety counseling. Risk wants a liability waiver before paying anything.
steps.q: How do you support fair resolution?
steps.hint: Remediation should not silence safety learning:

choices.label: Advocate remediation without gag coercion
choices.reaction: I can heal emotionally without signing away my voice.

choices.label: Push him to sign silence for cash
choices.reaction: You bought my anger — you didn't earn trust.

debrief.summary: Near-miss disclosure is still a serious honesty duty — patients deserve process transparency, sincere apology, and system fixes without coercive silence agreements. Blaming juniors or minimising facts destroys safety culture.
points: Harm includes betrayal of trust even without tissue injury.
Apology paired with facts is professional, not an admission of criminal guilt.
Incident reporting and patient communication serve different but linked duties.
Restitution should support recovery, not trade silence for money under pressure.
